input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10th-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements. Operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems where resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitate offloading computation for safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three services: Data Monitoring (real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks via UI; data saving and report generation); Computation Offloading (offloading ACC's lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance, and actuation commands to cloud infrastructure to alleviate on-board ECU constraints); Remote Management (remote enable/disable of ACC, desired speed setting, and emergency braking commands through UI). Target users are operators for monitoring/control and developers for troubleshooting using data collection capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for hardware-accelerated processing, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware for inter-component communication, and a SQL database for structured storage of object tracks and ego vehicle state data.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading between devices, which is less stringent than the stricter-than-100 ms requirements of safety-critical vehicular standards due to hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) are deployed on separate hardware components. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as a ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes, with the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. The Physical Twin Entity departs from the standard by hosting general automotive functionality (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) and is deployed on different hardware than the Digital Twin and User Entities, with all entities coordinated via 100 ms clock synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system includes Data Storage which gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking processes raw LiDAR data, Report Generation for analytical outputs, and ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) for vehicle control functions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, Motor, and Steering Servo. The Actuation component converts desired speed into motor voltage commands, with control mechanisms managing motor and steering servo operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, collecting ego vehicle speed, steering angle, and detected objects data as specified in Requirement R2.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, object tracks) is collected by Data Collecting FE, preprocessed via Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking and Data Pre-Processing FE components, and serialized by Data Translation for message type alignment and unit conversion. Real-time data flows through ROS2 network between software components and over Wi-Fi using UDP transport layer for inter-entity communication to enable 3D visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for Motor Actuation and Steering Servo Actuation are generated by the Controlling FE and processed through Data Translation. Control signals are transmitted via Wi-Fi using UDP protocol to physical actuators. The Control Panel User Interface FE provides the interface for inputting control commands. Specific command formats, validation, authentication, and feedback mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs via ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer. Data Translation handles serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversion for all data streams. Bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with ISO 23247:2021 standard, which includes four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture, Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) is referenced as an example for implementing data collection and device control functional entities.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the physical twin's nature (1/10th-scale, teleoperated), structural components (chassis layers, autonomy elements), and operational domain (ACC with ECU constraints). This is strongly supported by specific details in Section III.B and III.A. Quotes: '1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'retrofitting it with autonomy components', 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The description comprehensively lists the three specific services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with their detailed capabilities (visualization, cloud offloading of ACC logic, emergency braking) and target users (operators, developers). This aligns perfectly with the text in Section III.A. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading: ... determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', 'Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific software tools and frameworks used. It identifies 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0' as the SDK for the Jetson, 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)' as the middleware, and a 'SQL database' for storage. These details align perfectly with the descriptions of the implementation in the text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately captures the specific clock synchronization requirement and its justification found in the requirements section. It correctly identifies the 100 ms accuracy requirement, the comparison to safety-critical standards, and the reason for the specific value (hardware limitations) for computation offloading.

Supporting quotes:
- 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.'
- 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'
- 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The description correctly identifies the three main entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) and their deployment on separate hardware. It accurately describes the implementation details of Functional Entities as ROS2 packages and the specific decomposition of the User Interface FE into two subcomponents.

Supporting quotes:
- 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'
- 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes'
- 'the singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.'

The extraction provides a comprehensive overview of the system architecture, correctly citing the ISO 23247 RA foundation and the specific deviations made for the automotive case study. It accurately details the mapping of components to FEs, the unique role of the Physical Twin Entity hosting general functionality, and the deployment strategy involving hardware separation and time synchronization.

Supporting quotes:
- 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase'
- 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... the Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality'
- 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.'

The characteristic description requires information on 'integration with external systems... interoperability standards... dependencies on external systems... integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems.' The provided source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin system (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) and its internal communication (ROS2, Wi-Fi). While the abstract mentions 'cloud-based fleet coordination' as a general industry interest, the specific case study implementation described in the text does not detail integration with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins beyond the immediate vehicle-DT-user loop. Thus, 'Not in Document' is an accurate assessment regarding the specific implementation details required.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies specific software components and their functions as described in Table I and the text. It correctly lists 'Data Storage' with its SQL database function, 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' with its processing role, 'Report Generation', and the 'ACC' control logic. Quotes: 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed'.

The extraction precisely names the hardware components and describes the actuation logic found in the source. It correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and summarizes the function of the 'Actuation' component. Quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed of the vehicle'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific sensors ('Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera') and accurately links them to the data collection requirements specified in the text (R2). Quotes: 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'The DTS shall collect the following ACCrelevant data in real-time: ego vehicle’s speed; ego vehicle’s steering angle; detected objects'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document mentions 'high-fidelity' as a general definition of DTs and notes a fidelity constraint regarding the '1/10-scale' vehicle, it does not provide the specific details required by the characteristic definition, such as validation/verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection and correction mechanisms. The lack of this specific technical depth supports the 'Not in Document' assessment.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

Controlling FE', and 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands. User Interface FE'.

Therefore, the assessment that the detailed hosting information is missing is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions general lifecycle phases such as 'design phase' and 'vehicle operation' in Section II.B and discusses 'development' of the specific DT case study. However, it does not describe the specific lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin, representation types at different phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches as defined in the characteristic description. Since the extraction correctly identifies the absence of this detailed information, the score is 5.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source document details the implementation of a specific case study (ACC DT) but does not describe general engineering and development processes, quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies for Digital Twins as a characteristic. The text focuses on architecture and specific service implementation rather than the broader twinning process and evolution. Thus, the 'Not in Document' extraction is accurate.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document describes specific services like 'Data Monitoring' and 'Remote Management' (Section III.A) which involve displaying data and user commands, it does not describe the types of insights generated (other than raw data visualization), analytics and processing capabilities, decision-making processes (beyond manual user input), or confidence measures for recommendations. The characteristic requires a description of decision support capabilities which is not detailed in the text, supporting the 'Not in Document' extraction.

The extraction accurately describes the ISO 23247:2021 standard compliance and the specific parts of the standard, citing 'Overview and general principles', 'Reference architecture', 'Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements', and 'Information exchange'. The source text confirms this: 'The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4) Information exchange.' Additionally, the extraction correctly references OPC UA as an example, supported by the text: 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'. The extraction is specific and fully grounded in the sources.

The source text discusses data collection, storage, and reporting (e.g., 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle', 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports'), but it does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, or consent management. The text mentions that the system is 'safety-critical' and establishes a requirement for low latency: 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.' Thus, the extraction 'Not in Document' is accurate.
